data.title: MannKind gains FDA nod to expand inhaled insulin for children
data.description:
• Shares of MannKind Corporation (MNKD) spiked on Friday after the U.S. Food and Drug Administration approved the company’s request to expand its inhaled insulin therapy, Afrezza, for children with diabetes.
  • The inhalation powder developed using MannKind’s (MNKD) Technosphere drug-delivery platform is designed to deliver insulin directly into the blood through the lungs.
  • Used at the start of food intake, the rapid-acting needle-free insulin option was initially approved by the FDA for adults with diabetes mellitus in 2014.
  • The label expansion supported by the company’s pivotal INHALE‑1 clinical trial allows its use for patients aged six and older with type 1 and type 2 diabetes.
  • Afrezza, administered using a small, portable inhaler, is now available for eligible patients, the company said, adding that its pricing starts at $35 per month.

data.title: BP shares break six-session losing streak
data.description:
BP (BP) shares snapped six straight sessions of losses, as the stock was up 1% at $42.01 on Friday.

The oil and gas company fell nearly 8% in the preceding six sessions. The stock has advanced about 20% so far this year, compared to a 10.5% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index (SP500).

BP is down 10% over the past one month.

Recently, RBC Capital upgraded BP, saying the current windfall from higher crude prices presents "a second chance for BP to de-leverage and should help put the company on firmer footing for the coming years."

Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating, BP has a Hold rating with a score of 3.5 out of 5. The company received an A+ in the prospect of profitability, while it got a D in growth factor.

Turning to the Wall Street community, nine analysts gave BP a Buy and above rating. Six analysts have given the stock a Hold recommendation, while three recommended Sell or lower.

 
 

Seeking Alpha analysts are cautious and see the stock as a Hold.

data.title: Amazon dips as the Blue Origin rocket explosion sets back its global satellite plan
data.description:
Amazon (AMZN) underperformed the broad market on Friday as investors may be accounting for the impact of the massive Blue Origin New Glenn explosion at Cape Canaveral just days before it was due to launch 48 Amazon low-Earth-orbit broadband satellites. The explosion has reportedly seriously damaged the launchpad used by Blue Origin.  

Analysts note that the development primarily hurts Amazon (AMZN) through schedule risk, launch diversification, and perception rather than immediate financial damage.  

Roth Capital noted that Amazon's (AMZN) LEO constellation deployment timeline and direct-to-device commercial service expectations are likely to slip. Because SpaceX's (SPACE) Starlink (STRLK) is already far ahead in deployments, any delay in adding dozens of satellites slows Amazon's (AMZN)  progress toward global coverage and reinforces the sense that it is still playing catch-up, especially in high-value markets where density of satellites matters. Amazon (AMZN) had counted on up to 24 New Glenn launches as a backbone of its constellation rollout, so a prolonged stand-down reduces its planned annual deployment and could push more mass onto other, generally more expensive providers such as ULA and Ariane.

Shares of Amazon (AMZN) were down 1.1% in Friday afternoon trading.

data.title: Chevron CEO says Venezuela needs better contract terms to attract investment
data.description:
Chevron (CVX) CEO Mike Wirth said Venezuela needs to lower its taxes and royalties on oil to attract new investment.

“We need a new set of fiscal terms under which we would invest in the country,” Wirth told Bloomberg TV on Friday. “Right now the amount of tax and royalty that’s paid doesn’t leave enough for an investor to get a return on their investments.”

Bloomberg noted that Chevron currently only invests money that it makes within Venezuela as part of a U.S. program to recover debt from the country's state-owned oil company, Petroleos de Venezuela SA. Wirth said he sees the debt being paid in full within a year.

Meanwhile, American oil giants Chevron, Exxon Mobil (XOM), and ConocoPhillips (COP) have been meeting with Venezuelan officials to discuss terms and contracts following recent changes to the country's oil and gas laws, according to Bloomberg.

“There are negotiations underway, discussions even this week,” Wirth told Bloomberg. “I expect over the next short period of time we may see some clarity from them on specific values, on corporate income tax, on a range of things on royalties, and how that might be applied.”

Chevron plans to boost production in Venezuela by 50% over the next two years, using local profits to pay the costs, but would need more clarity before it considers committing additional capital.

“We don’t have enough clarity right now,” Wirth said. “We don’t understand what the regime would look like. And so it’s unlikely we would put capital to work until those things are clarified.”

data.title: META snaps six sessions of gains
data.description:
Shares of Meta Platforms (META) fell 1.77% to $624.06 on Friday, ending a six-session winning streak.

The stock had gained around 5% between May 20 and May 28, while the S&P 500 rose 1.76% over the same period.

Shares gained amid investors reacting to a wave of AI-related developments, including reports of AI chatbot subscription testing, continued cost-cutting measures to fund AI investments, and workforce restructuring initiatives. Sentiment was also supported by Meta’s dividend declaration, expansion of AI infrastructure partnerships, and broader enthusiasm surrounding mega-cap AI and hyperscaler stocks.

According to Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating system, META is rated a Hold with a score of 3.48 out of 5, with an A+ in terms of profitability but an F in terms of valuation.

A recent Seeking Alpha analysis said Meta Platforms is accelerating its artificial intelligence push to strengthen its competitive position across social media and advertising, with AI increasingly embedded across its core platforms and product strategy.

The note highlighted efforts to expand engagement and monetization through AI-driven features and new community-focused products, adding that “Meta wants to position itself as a leader in this space, and I believe that it is well-positioned to do so.”

On Wall Street, analysts are bullish, with 57 out of 64 analysts rating the stock with a buy or higher and seven suggesting to hold.

Shares have fallen about 6.9% over the past month and have lost around 4.81% year to date.

data.title: Okta expects Q2 FY2027 9% revenue growth with 26% non-GAAP operating margin as it scales AI agents push
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Okta, Inc. (OKTA) Q1 FY2027

Management View

  • "We're pleased with the strong start to FY '27" and "our results were driven by strength with large enterprises, partner engagement and contribution from our newer products" (Co-Founder, Chairman & CEO Todd McKinnon).

  • "Okta for AI agents, which became generally available last month" was positioned as a new control plane: "gives enterprises a single control plane to discover, govern and manage agents across their organization" (CEO McKinnon). On market timing, he emphasized: "It's still early. They're not materially contributing to the business in Q1" (CEO McKinnon).

  • Management tied broader platform motion to new products: "Our new product portfolio represented approximately 25% of Q1 bookings" and "We see a 40% ACV uplift when new products are included in a deal" (CEO McKinnon).

  • Partnerships were highlighted as part of the AI-agents strategy, including: "a partnership with ServiceNow," "Our partnership with Google," "integrates with Amazon Bedrock Agent core," "a launch partner for OpenAI's release of GPT 5.5 trusted access for cyber" and "collaborating with Anthropic" (CEO McKinnon).

  • "We had another strong quarter of cash flow in Q1" and "ended the quarter with... approximately $2.6 billion in cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments" (Chief Financial Officer Brett Tighe).

  • Capital allocation updates included: "we will settle the remaining principal amount of $350 million in cash" for convertibles, and "we repurchased and retired just over 3 million shares for a total cost of $241 million" with "$680 million" remaining under the "$1 billion repurchase program" (CFO Tighe).

Outlook

  • "For the second quarter of FY '27, we expect total revenue growth of 9%, current RPO growth of 11%, non-GAAP operating margin of 26% and free cash flow margin of 20% to 21%" (CFO Tighe).

  • "For the full year FY '27, we now expect total revenue growth of 9% to 10%, non-GAAP operating margin of 25% to 26% and a free cash flow margin of 27% to 28%" (CFO Tighe).

  • Management reiterated a prior guidance item while adding timing: "the FY '27 revenue guidance includes about a 1 point impact related to a strategic decision to shift more of our professional services business to our partners" and "is expected to start to materialize in Q2" (CFO Tighe).

Financial Results

  • Core operating indicators referenced on the call included: "our 12% revenue growth," "net retention inflecting up to 107" and "cRPO of 12%" (CEO McKinnon).

  • The quarter’s mix and go-to-market signals emphasized: "positive go-to-market KPI improvements, including increased sales productivity, strong pipeline build and low AE attrition" (CFO Tighe), alongside "partner-sourced bookings experienced a meaningful increase including multiple million dollars plus deals in Q1" (CFO Tighe).

  • Customer mix concentration was updated: "Large customers represent 85% of ACV now" (Joshua Tilton, Wolfe Research, LLC: Question) and management attributed it to "the result of the hard work that we've been doing over the last few years" (CFO Tighe).

Q&A

  • John DiFucci, Guggenheim Securities, LLC, Research Division: asked how AI agents are materializing in customer environments; CEO McKinnon said "everyone is deploying agents," but many did so "in a haphazard nonsecure way" and are now "figuring out those rails," adding "it's still early" and "not materially contributing to the business in Q1."

  • Brian Essex, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: asked if heightened threat narratives are changing budgets and sales cycles; CEO McKinnon said customers are "very interested" in new model intelligence, but "they're panicking and changing their priorities... is not true," framing demand as "the fundamentals" and "accelerating the importance of investing in your identity infrastructure."

  • Eric Heath, KeyBanc Capital Markets Inc., Research Division: asked about pricing strategy and early uplift; CEO McKinnon said pricing is "an uplift to a named user" or "an uplift to a monthly active user" and "we're pricing for market share and reducing friction," while CFO Tighe added "The average deal size for these AI-specific deals is significantly larger than the average deal size for the rest of the company."

  • Adam Tindle, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division: asked about AI pipeline scale and relative progress between Okta vs Auth0 AI agents; CEO McKinnon said "The pipeline is bigger than anything we've ever seen" and "They're both healthy, the Okta pipeline is bigger."

  • Yun Suk Kim, Loop Capital Markets LLC, Research Division: pressed on whether AI line items are small; CFO Tighe clarified "I'm only talking about the AI line on the deal" and "the line item itself is a pretty large line item," while CEO McKinnon said scaling is "about doing more deals" because "The deals are big."

  • Fatima Boolani, Citigroup Inc., Research Division: questioned the services shift vs peers leaning into services; CEO McKinnon said "our services team is evolving" into "architectural consultative" work while GSIs "help us just get the scale," and CFO Tighe tied the move to partners, citing "partner source ARR in Q1 did really well."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were focused and probing on AI agents’ timing, differentiation, pricing units, and budget dynamics, including skepticism about market noise: "it all sounds pretty similar" and CISOs are "confused" (Gray Powell, BTIG, LLC: Question).

  • Management tone was confident on positioning but cautious on near-term revenue contribution from AI agents, repeatedly using language such as "it's still early" and "prudent" (CEO McKinnon; CFO Tighe).

  • Compared with the prior quarter, management continued to emphasize AI agents as a long-term growth vector, but Q1 added more near-term execution signals (pipeline, deal-size comments, and partnerships) while keeping the same prudence on near-term materiality (CEO McKinnon; CFO Tighe).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • New products contribution shifted from "approximately 30% of Q4 bookings" in the prior quarter to "approximately 25% of Q1 bookings" in the current quarter (CEO McKinnon).

  • AI agents narrative moved from early access and deal examples ("Okta for AI Agents... available in early access in January") to commercialization milestones and integrations ("became generally available last month" and integrations with ServiceNow, Google, Amazon Bedrock) (CEO McKinnon).

  • Guidance language stayed consistent on prudence, while Q1 reiterated and time-qualified the professional services shift: prior quarter framed "about a 1-point impact" from moving services to partners; current quarter added it "is expected to start to materialize in Q2" (CFO Tighe).

Risks and Concerns

  • AI agents monetization timing risk was explicitly acknowledged: "It's still early" and "They're not materially contributing to the business in Q1" (CEO McKinnon).

  • Pricing-model uncertainty was noted as the market evolves: "it's very hard to define the number of agents" and the industry is "figuring that out" (CEO McKinnon), with guardrails on experimentation such as "There's no unlimited" (CEO McKinnon).

  • Cash flow sensitivity to capital allocation was flagged in guidance context: FY'27 free cash flow margin includes "about a 1 point impact related to lower interest income" tied to repurchases and settling notes in cash (CFO Tighe).

Final Takeaway

Okta management described Q1 FY2027 as a strong start led by large enterprises, partners, and newer products, while positioning AI agents as a major, longer-duration opportunity that is already reshaping strategic conversations but is still not material to current-quarter results. The company reiterated a prudent outlook while highlighting expanding AI-agent partnerships, large early AI-agent deal sizes, a healthy cash position, ongoing buybacks, and an accelerating shift of professional services work toward global systems integrators to support scale.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Heinz snaps six sessions of gains
data.description:
Shares of The Kraft Heinz Company (KHC) fell 1.08% to $24.21 on Friday, ending a six-session winning streak.

The stock had gained around 4% between May 20 and May 28, while the S&P 500 rose 1.76% over the same period.

Shares rose amid investor-focused developments from The Kraft Heinz Company, including progress on its up to $1.1 billion cash tender offer for outstanding notes, with the company announcing pricing terms, accepted tender amounts, early participation results, satisfaction of financing conditions, and an early settlement election.

The company also announced its participation in the upcoming Deutsche Bank dbAccess Global Consumer Conference.

According to Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating system, KHC is rated a Hold with a score of 3.28 out of 5, with an A+ in terms of valuation but an F in terms of growth.

A recent analysis on The Kraft Heinz Company said the company appears significantly undervalued despite ongoing market share pressures and earnings weakness, with multiple valuation models implying substantial upside from current levels.

The note highlighted that Kraft Heinz has been “losing market share in the US for some time now,” but argued that “a lot of bad news seems to be already baked into the stock price,” adding that “all that KHC needs to do is deliver stabilization to create a positive upward momentum.”

On Wall Street, analysts are cautious, with one out of 20 analysts rating the stock with a buy or higher, 15 suggesting to hold, and four of them recommending a sell or lower.

Shares rose about 7.6% over the past month and have gained around 0.7% year to date.

data.title: Activists protest French government's added stake in TotalEnergies
data.description:
Climate activists demonstrated against TotalEnergies (TTE) on Friday to protest the French government's accumulation of a ‌1.33% stake worth $2.8B in the company, which began its annual shareholders meeting.

"The government must stop protecting TotalEnergies, stop all new ⁠investments with our funds in these fossil-fuel projects, and finally tax these superprofits," Laure Fourquet of non-profit Avaaz told Reuters.

The French ​government stake in TotalEnergies (TTE) is held through the Caisse des Depots et Consignements, the state's financial arm handling regulated ​savings accounts that guarantee citizens a rate of return.

The company has been criticized for its large profits resulting from higher oil prices due to the Middle East war, with French politicians pushing for a supertax to subsidize energy aid for consumers.

Chairman and CEO Patrick Pouyanne said at the annual general ​meeting that French citizens would rather see ​energy companies cap fuel prices at ‌the pump than pay a superprofit tax.

"I believe that's ​why ⁠the government walked back its intention to impose a supertax," Pouyanne said. "We are the only company to voluntarily ⁠cap ​our fuel prices in France, ​and the more we sell the more we lose."

Additionally, billionaire Daniel Kretinsky said he is open to increasing his holding in TotalEnergies (TTE) after recently acquiring a 4.2% stake.

data.title: Here's why Viasat slipped after 132% YTD rally: higher targets post Q4 were not enough
data.description:
Viasat (VSAT) stock price dropped over ~8% on Friday to ~$79.44, as investors locked in profits after the stock delivered a massive ~132.59% YTD gain, sharply outperforming the broader S&P 500 (SP500) return of 10.49%.

The pullback came after the satellite communications company posted improved Q4 FY2026 results and received fresh bullish calls from Wall Street analysts.

Viasat reported Q4 revenue of $1.17B, up 2% YoY, while net income came in at $58.8M as compared to a loss of $246.1M last year. The turnaround was largely supported by a $168.1M gain from the sale of its Navarino investment.

Moreover, its adjusted EBITDA slipped slightly to $369.9M from $374.8M, while new contract awards rose 9% YoY to $1.28B.

For the full year, revenue reached a record $4.64B, up 3% YoY, while adjusted EBITDA held steady at a record $1.55B. Importantly, the company generated positive free cash flow for the fifth straight quarter.

The company also reduced total debt to $6.59B and ended the year with $2.9B in liquidity.

While headline numbers looked stronger, investors appeared cautious around slower near-term profitability growth and flat FY2027 EBITDA guidance. 

The company expects FY2027 adjusted EBITDA to remain flat to slightly higher YoY, while free cash flow guidance of nearly $180M, excluding non-recurring Ligado payments, also disappointed some traders after the stock’s sharp run-up this year.

Still, it showed strength in other areas. Defense and Advanced Technologies revenue climbed 12% YoY to $361M, while full-year contract awards hit a record $4.93B, with backlog rising 15% to $4.07B.

Wall Street stayed optimistic after earnings. B. Riley reportedly raised its price target on Viasat to $106 from $94 with a Buy rating, citing growing momentum in defense, spectrum, and satellite services. 

Meanwhile, according to TheFly, Raymond James increased its target to $93 from $74 and maintained an Outperform rating, highlighting upside from the company’s Defense and Advanced Technologies business and potential strategic spin-off plans.

data.title: Asana expects FY 2027 revenue of $855.5M-$863.5M as it adds StackAI and guides Q2 revenue to $213M-$215M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Asana (ASAN) Q1 FY 2027

Management View

  • “We delivered a strong start to the year with revenue of $205.1 million, up 9.5% year-over-year and above the high end of our guidance,” said CEO Daniel Rogers, adding, “we’re seeing positive trends across customer retention, expansion and AI product adoption.”

  • Rogers highlighted retention and vertical momentum, saying “overall in-quarter NRR improved for the fourth consecutive quarter to 97%,” and added, “in the technology sector, we returned to positive year-over-year growth for the first time in 8 quarters.”

  • On AI monetization and product traction, Rogers said, “Taken together, our AI product bookings now represent 17% of net new ARR in Q1,” and “the number of customers spending over $100,000 annually on AI Studio nearly doubled.”

  • Rogers announced M&A, stating, “today, we’re announcing the acquisition of StackAI,” and described the rationale as, “StackAI is the logical evolution of AI Studio and accelerates our road map by over a year.”

  • “Q1 revenues were at $205.1 million, up 9.5% year-over-year,” CFO Aziz Megji said, while also noting, “We delivered an 11.5% non-GAAP operating margin or $23.6 million of operating income.”

Outlook

  • Megji guided Q2 revenue to “$213 million to $215 million,” and said the range “includes an expected contribution from StackAI of approximately 50 basis points to growth.”

  • Megji guided Q2 profitability to “non-GAAP operating income…$18 million to $20 million,” and “non-GAAP net income per share…$0.08 to $0.09.”

  • For FY 2027, Megji forecast revenue of “$855.5 million to $863.5 million,” with “non-GAAP operating margin of at least 9.75%,” and “non-GAAP net income of $0.37 per share.”

  • Versus the prior quarter’s framing, Megji said, “the core assumptions underlying the FY ’27 outlook we shared last quarter remain largely unchanged,” including that “PLG remains a near-term headwind,” and guidance “does not factor in the improvement we have seen in the tech vertical over the past two quarters.”

  • Analysts’ estimates were provided in the prompt, but the fiscal-quarter format does not match the required Q1/Q2/Q3/Q4 validation, so no estimates comparison is included.

Financial Results

  • Megji reported customer scale and mix, stating, “We have 26,103 core customers,” and “this cohort represented 76% of our revenues in Q1,” alongside “817 customers spending $100,000 or more on an annualized basis.”

  • On retention, Megji said, “Our overall dollar-based net retention rate was 96%,” and added, “our NRR is a trailing 4-quarter average and therefore, a lagging indicator of more recent trends.”

  • On profitability and timing items, Megji said, “Our gross margin was 88%,” and added that results “benefited from approximately $3 million of operating expenses that shifted from Q1 into the second half.”

  • On earnings and cash generation, Megji reported, “Net income was $24.4 million or $0.10 per share,” and “adjusted free cash flow was $34.4 million in the quarter or 17% of revenue.”

  • On capital allocation and liquidity, Megji said, “we bought back $45 million…at an average price of $6.11 per share,” and ended Q1 with “approximately $424.6 million” in cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities.

  • On StackAI deal terms and margin impact, Megji said the transaction includes “approximately $75 million in upfront cash consideration,” and “StackAI is expected to represent approximately a 1 percentage point drag on operating margins in Q2 and the second half of FY ’27.”

Q&A

  • Robert Oliver, Baird: asked why buy StackAI now and integration speed; CEO Rogers: said customers wanted automation “extend those workflows to third-party systems,” and added, “why not deliver it to our customers today?”

  • Oliver, Baird: asked about verticalized go-to-market and whether StackAI requires FTEs; CEO Rogers: tied GTM work to “multiproduct…multi-buying center,” plus “customer health” and “sales productivity,” and said focus is “better serving our ICP.”

  • Matthew Bullock, BofA: asked what drove tech vertical returning to growth and whether Anthropic contributed; CFO Megji: said it was “primarily expansion,” driven by “AI Studio…now…AI Teammates,” plus “seat expansion,” while noting, “We haven’t factored that into our guidance.”

  • Bullock, BofA: asked about $100,000+ customer count softness; CFO Megji: said it was “sequentially flat,” cited quarter-length effects, and added, “we tend to view that metric on a year-over-year basis.”

  • Patrick Walravens, Citizens JMP: asked how Asana breaks through agent “clutter”; CEO Rogers: emphasized differentiation in “Work Graph,” “multiplayer mode,” “shared memory,” and governance, and said messaging starts with “Work Innovation Summit on June 4.”

  • Jackson Ader, KeyBanc: asked if $100,000 AI Studio metric is AI Studio spend; CFO Megji: “they’re $100,000 on the SKU, AI Studio,” and added AI Studio spend is “a double-digit percentage” of core spend.

  • Ader, KeyBanc: asked StackAI buyer persona; CEO Rogers: pointed to “AI centers of excellence,” saying the buying center can be in “operations teams” or “IT teams.”

  • Steven Enders, Citi: asked how Studio adoption changes seat behavior and Teammates feedback; CFO Megji: said Studio adopters are “the strongest NRR cohort,” and for Teammates, “the pipe is growing fairly rapidly.”

  • Josh Baer, Morgan Stanley: asked what must happen to return to 100% NRR and reaccelerate; CEO Rogers: said levers include “multiproduct platform,” “customer health,” “sales productivity,” and “operating velocity.”

  • William Fitzsimmons, Piper Sandler: asked what’s driving NRR improvement; CFO Megji: said Q1 in-quarter NRR improvement to 97% was “coming both on GRR and expansion,” with “the larger piece…on expansion,” and noted “Next quarter, we have the large customer downgrade kind of laps off.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to neutral, pressing on competitive noise in AI agents, durability of tech-vertical recovery, and whether $100,000+ customer adds were stalling, including, “it’s just so noisy right now,” from Patrick Walravens (Citizens JMP).

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more defensive-cautious in guidance framing, repeatedly stressing prudence, including, “We haven’t factored that into our guidance,” and, “we need some additional positive inflection to get more constructive,” from CFO Megji.

  • Compared with the previous quarter, management maintained similar caution on PLG headwinds and tech stabilization assumptions, while the current call’s tone leaned more confident around AI monetization and added a major strategic action via StackAI.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • A key structural change was finance leadership: Q4 FY 2026 featured outgoing CFO Sonalee Parekh and announced Aziz Megji as incoming CFO; Q1 FY 2027 was Megji’s first call as CFO.

  • Q1 FY 2027 introduced a new strategy catalyst not present in Q4: Rogers announced “the acquisition of StackAI,” positioning it as cross-system orchestration that “accelerates our road map by over a year.”

  • Guidance moved up in phrasing and figures: FY 2027 revenue guidance increased to “$855.5 million to $863.5 million” (Q1) from “$850 million to $858 million” (Q4), and operating margin guidance rose to “at least 9.75%” from “at least 9.5%.”

  • Both quarters emphasized PLG as a headwind and tech stabilization as not embedded in outlook; Q1 reiterated, “guidance does not factor in the improvement…in the tech vertical,” consistent with Q4’s “too early to call a bottom” positioning.

Risks and Concerns

  • Megji said PLG remains a headwind, stating, “our guidance continues to assume approximately a 2-point drag on ARR growth from PLG,” tied to “AI search and LLM-driven experiences.”

  • StackAI integration and profitability risk was acknowledged via margin impact: “approximately a 1 percentage point drag on operating margins in Q2 and the second half of FY ’27,” according to Megji.

  • Dilution remained an explicit concern: “We recognize that stock-based compensation and dilution remain elevated,” Megji said, adding SBC assumptions “remain in the low 20s as a percentage of revenue for the fiscal year.”

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a combination of steady single-digit revenue growth, expanding profitability, and improving in-quarter retention dynamics, while making a major strategic move to acquire StackAI to extend AI workflow orchestration across enterprise systems; guidance kept PLG as a headwind and excluded recent tech-vertical improvement, while emphasizing AI products’ growing contribution and a planned deeper AI outlook update on the Q2 call.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
